quotes.verbatim_quote:
Primarily dance oriented, the frevo is basically a march, with syncopated figures in
the melody and in the rhythmic patterns, usually up tempo.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the foundational rhythmic identity of frevo: syncopation and tempo context that governs all the guitar patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Frevo is usually written in 2/4 although you can find the use of 3/4 or 4/4. The
examples in this book are written in 4/4. The melodic accent comes on the up
beat and the melodies are pretty much syncopated.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives essential metric and accentuation rules for reading and performing frevo examples.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
bass part is much like a jazz “walking bass”, playing even quarter notes, and the
harmonies are quite simple, usually turnarounds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Cross-references frevo bass to jazz walking bass and notes turnaround-based harmony, shaping how to comp.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Keep in your mind that the syncopations are played on
the top voices of the chord, while the bass note comes on the beat.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core rule for basic frevo guitar pattern: prescribes which voices carry the syncopation and which carry the downbeat pulse.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
As in other Brazilian styles, the bass line keeps switching between the root and
the fifth of the chord, and it's a better choice to play the fifth below the root.
If the bass note is already placed on the 6th string, you may keep the same note
for the whole measure.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Governs left-hand voicing choices for every frevo chord, dictating the idiomatic bass motion.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Each variation can be played through the entire song or you can switch between
the variations to create a new pattern. It's also common to use a different pattern
at the end of a section or a "cadenza".
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pedagogical rule explaining structural deployment of variations across a performance.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In this variation you'll find a new syncopation in the 3rd beat.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Labels the technical difference introduced in Variation #1.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This is a good example of a variation playing only the up beats of the measure.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines Variation #4 as a fully up-beat texture, an important rhythmic concept in frevo accompaniment.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play this example using straight 8th notes, muting the down beats with
your left hand.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces a left-hand muting technique as an alternative execution for Variation #4.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This variation is an example of a “marcha rancho” in 3/4 meter.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Connects Variation #5 to marcha-rancho, showing how the 3/4 feel is realised on guitar.
